Part B.key_evidence: The science cutout shows a faint central point-like source, the reference cutout lacks a clear central counterpart or host, and the difference cutout shows a compact positive residual at the center. Metadata also indicate a point-like detection (classtar 0.976, fwhm 2.15), positive subtraction, and only a single historical detection (ndethist 1, deltajd 0) despite extensive coverage (ncovhist 1475). The nearest PS1 source is 9.7 arcsec away, so it is not a strong host or stellar counterpart for the alert position.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: This is most consistent with a real solar-system object (Asteroid in this benchmark). The combination of an isolated single-epoch positive point source, no clear persistent counterpart in the reference image, and ndethist=1 strongly favors a mover over a fixed astrophysical variable, AGN, or SN tied to a host.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A hostless or very faint-host supernova is the main astrophysical alternative, since the alert appears as a new positive source. That is weaker because there is no visible host/nuclear association, no persistent source at the position, and only one detection despite heavy prior coverage; a subtraction artifact is also less favored because the residual looks compact rather than dipolar or irregular.
